coredata.dc:title: Ultrahigh current efficiency of light-emitting devices based on octadecylamine-graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Kim, Dae Hun
Kim, Tae Whan
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have currently emerged as excellent candidates for applications in GQD-based LEDs because of their potential applications in next-generation multifunctional systems. However, the numerous oxygenous functional groups and defects existing in the GQDs restrict their florescence quantum yield, resulting in a decrease in the brightness of light-emitting devices (LEDs) based on GQDs. Here, we report on solution-processed and highly-efficient LEDs that have been developed based on octadecylamine (ODA)-GQDs. In contrast with the GQDs, the number of oxygen components in the ODA-GQDs is lower due to nucleophilic substitution between the amine and the epoxy functional groups. The current and the external quantum efficiency of an LED with ODA-GQDs are 6.51cd/A and 2.67%, respectively, this current efficiency of 6.51cd/A being the highest among the efficiencies reported for LEDs based on GQDs. The efficiency enhancement in the LEDs with ODA-GQDs is attributed to significant improvements in the optical properties of the ODA-GQDs. The successful demonstration of this efficiency enhancement for LEDs based on ODA-GQDs indicates a potential for applications of GQD-based LEDs as next-generation real optical sources.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dot
Graphene
Light-emitting device
Graphene quantum dot
Current efficiency

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Syntheses of ODA NPs and ODA-GQDs
Fabrication method of the LED with ODA-GQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Education, Science and Technology
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Education, Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education, Science and Technology (2016R1A2A1A05005502).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the processes for the syntheses of (a) the ODA NPs and (b) the ODA-GQDs.
C1s peaks for the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy spectra of the GO, the rGO, and the ODA-GQDs, and (b) Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy spectra of the rGO and the ODA-GQDs.
Raman spectra of the GO, the rGO, and the ODA-GQDs.
Low-resolution TEM image of the (a) ODA NPs and high-resolution TEM images of the (b) ODA NPs and the ODA– GQDs (c) before and (d) after irradiation by the electron microscope beam.
(a) PL and PL excitation spectra, (b) UPS spectra, and (c) energy-band diagrams and molecular structures of the GQDs and the ODA-GQDs.
Normalized PL decay as a function of time for the PVK, PVK:GQDs, and PVK:ODA-GQDs.

(a) Device structure, (b) schematic energy diagram, and (c) current density - voltage, (d) luminance - voltage, (e) current efficiency - luminance, and (f) normalized EL - wavelength curves for LEDs with PVK, PVK:ODA NPs, PVK:GQDs, and PVK:ODA-GQDs.

Comparison of the current efficiencies, external quantum efficiencies, and wavelengths between the light-emitting devices based on graphene quantum dots in this work with those in related references published in recent years.

head.ce:title: Ultrahigh current efficiency of light-emitting devices based on octadecylamine-graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electronic and Computer Engineering, Hanyang University, Seoul 04763, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Electronic and Computer Engineering, Hanyang University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have currently emerged as excellent candidates for applications in GQD-based LEDs because of their potential applications in next-generation multifunctional systems. However, the numerous oxygenous functional groups and defects existing in the GQDs restrict their florescence quantum yield, resulting in a decrease in the brightness of light-emitting devices (LEDs) based on GQDs. Here, we report on solution-processed and highly-efficient LEDs that have been developed based on octadecylamine (ODA)-GQDs. In contrast with the GQDs, the number of oxygen components in the ODA-GQDs is lower due to nucleophilic substitution between the amine and the epoxy functional groups. The current and the external quantum efficiency of an LED with ODA-GQDs are 6.51cd/A and 2.67%, respectively, this current efficiency of 6.51cd/A being the highest among the efficiencies reported for LEDs based on GQDs. The efficiency enhancement in the LEDs with ODA-GQDs is attributed to significant improvements in the optical properties of the ODA-GQDs. The successful demonstration of this efficiency enhancement for LEDs based on ODA-GQDs indicates a potential for applications of GQD-based LEDs as next-generation real optical sources.

ce:section.ce:para: Light-emitting devices (LEDs) have been extensively investigated due to their excellent advantages of low power consumption, high luminous efficiency, and long lifetime [1–3]. Organic LEDs have superior advantages of low cost and easy fabrication for large-scale production [4,5]. However, the luminescence stability of organic LEDs in a humid atmosphere is poor, and their lifetime is short [6,7]. Quantum-dot (QD) LEDs have been considered as next-generation optical sources due to their having a high fluorescent quantum-yield, narrow emission-bandwidth, and resistance to photobleaching. However, semiconductor QDs usually contain a toxic heavy metal, which hampers their rapid development [8–10]. Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), such as a small graphene fragment, have been receiving considerable attention for potential applications in next-generation optoelectronic devices due to their having tunable emission properties in the range of visible light [11]. GQDs have a non-zero band-gap, strong excitation luminesce due to the quantum confinement and the edge effects, simple band-gap tuning, low toxicity, high chemical stability, and excellent carrier transport mobility [12–16].

The various synthesis methods for GQDs have been divided into two broad groups of top-down and bottom-up methods [17]. Top-down methods include the decomposition and the exfoliation of readily-available bulk graphene-based materials under harsh conditions. The top-down methods typically require several steps, which are the utilization of concentrated acids, the strong oxidation control of the morphology, and the size distribution of the particles. Top-down methods employ hydrothermal cutting [18,19], dissolved thermal cutting [20], electrochemical cutting [15,21], nanolithography [12], nanotomy-assisted exfoliation [22], and ultrasonic shearing [23]. However, bottom-up methods include the synthesis of QDs from polycyclic aromatic compounds or molecules with aromatic structures, which allow for excellent control of the physical and the optical properties of the GQDs. The bottom-up methods involve stepwise organic synthesis [24] and the cage opening of fullerenes [25]. Even though several methods for the synthesis of GQDs have been suggested, those methods require special equipment and extremely expensive raw materials, resulting in long fabrication time, low production yield, and utilization of harmful chemical reagents. The method of the oxidation, with a further cutting reaction, of the graphite is appealing for the mass production of GQDs [26,27]. However, numerous oxygenous functional groups and defects are inherently generated both on the basal plane and at the edges of the GQDs, which significantly affect the dispersion properties of the GQDs, make control of their emission wavelength difficult, and decrease the intensity of the luminance quantum yield [28,29]. Oxidation of the GQDs prevents us from acquiring both a fundamental understanding of the origin of the luminescence and a realization of practical applications.

Here, we demonstrate LEDs based on octadecylamine (ODA)-GQDs formed by using a simple solution method, these LEDS having the highest current efficiency among the reported LEDs based on GQDs. The ODA-GQDs formed by using a safe method of synthesis are cost-effective and have eco-friendly advantages due to the presence of low oxygenous functional groups. The efficiency enhancement of the LEDs with ODA-GQDs is attributed to the improved hole injection efficiency from the anode to the active layer due to the reduced hole barrier and the optimized energy-band matching in the LEDs with ODA-GQDs as the active layer, resulting in the achievement of the highest current efficiency among those reported for LEDs based on GQDs. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were performed to investigate the microstructural properties of the ODA-GQDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and Raman spectroscopy measurements were performed to investigate nucleophilic substitution in the ODA-GQDs. Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) and photoluminescence (PL) measurements were carried out to investigate the optical properties of the ODA-GQDs. Current density - voltage - luminance measurements were carried out to investigate the electrical and the optical properties of the LEDs fabricated utilizing ODA-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Syntheses of ODA NPs and ODA-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The ODA-GQDs were synthesized by using a simple solution method. Fig. 1 shows a schematic diagram of the processes for the syntheses of the ODA nanoparticles (NPs) and the ODA-GQDs used in this study. Firstly, the synthesis process for the ODA NPs is described in Fig. 1a. ODA powder is dissolved in a mixture of chlorobenzene and methanol (0.05mg/mL). The mixed solution is stirred by using an ultrasonic method for 1h, resulting in the formation of ODA NPs. The formation of ODA NPs is attributed to intra-chain aggregates of ODA molecules due to the polar difference between chlorobenzene and methanol. Secondly, the synthesis method for the ODA-GQDs is presented in Fig. 1b. A mixture of ODA and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) is dissolved in a methanol solution at a mass fraction ratio of 20:1. The ODA and the rGO are maintained in a mixed solution for 24h, after which an ODA-rGO flake is formed in the methanol solution. Nucleophilic substitution occurs between the amine functional groups of the ODA and the epoxy functional groups of the rGO. The product is then washed with methanol to remove the excess ODA adsorbed on the surface of the ODA-rGO flake. The resultant powder is dried under vacuum at 100°C. After the ODA-rGO flake has been dissolved in a mixed solution of chlorobenzene and methanol with a volume ratio of 50:1, the ODA-rGO solution is mixed for 3h by using an ultrasonic method. An abrupt reaction between the ODA-rGO flake and the chlorobenzene not only exfoliates the ODA-rGO flakes but also breaks the ODA-rGO pieces into GQDs. High-quality ODA-GQDs are formed due to the polarity difference in the mixed solution and to the aggregation of ODA chains of ODA-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication method of the LED with ODA-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The sheet resistivity and the thickness of the ITO thin films coated on the glass substrates were 15ohm/square and 150nm, respectively. The ITO-coated glass substrates were cleaned in acetone and methanol at 25°C for 20min by using an ultrasonic cleaner and were thoroughly rinsed in de-ionized water. After the chemically-cleaned ITO-coated glass substrates had been dried by using N2 gas with a purity of 99.99%, the surfaces of the ITO-coated glass substrates were treated with an ultraviolet-ozone gas for 20min at room temperature. After the PEDOT:PSS layer had been deposited onto the ITO-coated glass substrates, the ODA-GQD solution was deposited onto the PEDOT:PSS/ITO-coated glass substrates by using a spin-coating method at 5000rpm for 41s and was then annealed at 145°C for 15min in a glove box. After the samples had been transferred into an evaporation chamber, the organic layers and the electrodes were deposited at a substrate temperature of 25°C and a system pressure of 1.7×10−4 Pa. The deposition rates of the organic layers and the metal layer were approximately 1.0 and 2.0Å/s, respectively.

The TEM images were measured by using a JEM 2100F transmission electron microscope operating at 200kV. The XPS spectra were measured by using a theta probe base system purchased from the Thermo Fisher Scientific Co. The Raman measurements were obtained by using an IS50 model spectrometer. The PL measurements were performed by using an X-ray fluorescence spectrometer (TCSPC-FL920) equipped with a pulsed laser operating at 365nm. The UPS spectra were measured by using a theta probe base system (Thermo Fisher Scientific Co.). The UPS spectra were obtained at a system pressure of 6.7×10−10 Pa with a HeI 21.2-eV source. The current-voltage characteristics were measured on a programmable electrometer with built-in current and voltage measurement units (M6100, McScience). The brightness intensities were measured by using a brightness meter, and the EL spectra were measured by using a luminescence spectrometer (CS-1000, Minolta). All measurements of the electrical characteristics for the LEDs were carried out at 25°C under atmospheric conditions.

Fig. 2 shows C1s XPS and FTIR spectra of GO, rGO, and ODA-GQDs. The dominant peaks at 284.5, 286.5, 287.1, and 288.1eV for the XPS spectrum correspond to the C-C bond of GO and to the presence of the oxygen functional groups of the carbon atoms for the C-OH bond, C-O-C bond, and C˭O bond, respectively, as shown in Fig. 2a. In contrast to the rGO, the peak intensities related to the oxygen functional groups of the carbon atoms for the XPS spectra of the ODA-GQDs are decreased. The decreased peak intensities of the oxygen functional groups for the carbon atoms originate from the nucleophilic substitution between the amine functional groups of the ODA and the epoxy functional groups of the rGO. Fig. 2b shows the FTIR spectra of the rGO and the ODA-GQDs. The FTIR peaks related to the C-H band at 2922cm−1 and the C˭O/COOH band at 1646cm−1 are observed in the spectra from both the rGO and the ODA-GQDs. The C-N band at 1012cm−1 and the C˭N band at 1549cm−1 appear in the spectrum for the ODA-GQDs. The intensity of the epoxide band at 1064cm−1 for the ODA-GQDs is smaller than that for the rGO. The FTIR and the XPS spectra indicate the existence of nucleophilic substitution between the amine functional groups of the ODA and the epoxy functional groups of the rGO. The epoxy groups might destroy the C-C bonds, resulting in non-radiative recombination of localized excitons.

Fig. 3 shows the Raman spectra of the GO, the rGO, and the ODA-GQDs. While the D band at 1355cm−1 for the Raman spectrum of the GO is related to local defects and disorders that break the symmetry and the selection rule, the G band at 1594cm−1 typically appears due to the in-plane carbon-atom stretching vibrations, as shown in Fig. 3a. The peak position of the G band for rGO shifts slightly toward a lower wave number from 1594 to 1584cm−1, indicative of the self-healing effect of rGO, which recovers the hexagonal network of carbon atoms with defects, as shown in Fig. 3b [30]. The shift of the G band's peak position clarifies the reduction of GO to rGO. The position of the G band for the ODA-GQDs shifts toward higher wave number from 1584 to 1587cm−1 due to the exfoliation of the GQDs from the rGO, as shown in Fig. 3c. The D-to-G peak intensity (ID/IG) ratio is used to investigate the structural disorder. The ID/IG ratios for the GO, the rGO, and the ODA-GQDs are 0.88, 0.93, and 1.38, respectively. An increase in the ID/IG ratio from 0.88 to 0.93 indicates the removal of the oxide functional groups attached to the surface of the GO during the reduction from GO to rGO [31]. The ID/IG intensity ratio of the ODA-GQDs (1.38) is larger than that of the rGO (0.93), indicative of a decrease in the number of oxide functional groups due to the nucleophilic substitution between the amine functional groups of the ODA and the epoxy functional groups of the rGO, resulting in the formation of high-quality GQDs with a low number of oxygen groups in the ODA-GQDs.

Fig. 4a shows a low-resolution TEM image of the ODA NPs. The ODA NPs are formed by the aggregation of ODA chains due to the polarity difference in the mixed solution [32]. The diameter of the ODA NPs is approximately 20nm, and the NPs do not exhibit fringe patterns, as shown in Fig. 4b. Fig. 4c shows a high-resolution TEM image of the ODA-GQDs before irradiation by the electron microscope's beam. The particle sizes of the ODA-GQDs are in the range between 25 and 35nm. The ODA-GQDs are synthesized by the bonding of the ODA chains on the GQDs due to the polarity difference between the solvents. Fig. 4d shows a high-resolution TEM image of the the ODA-GQDs after irradiation by the electron microscope's beam. When the ODA-GQDs are irradiated by the electron microscope's beam at 200kV, the variation in the ODA-GQDs increases with increasing exposure time (Fig. S1). The volume of the ODA-GQDs decreases with increasing exposure time due to the loss of ODA molecules, resulting in the confirmation of the dispersion degree for the GQDs in the ODA-GQDs. The size of the GQDs is approximately 4.2nm, and the lattice fringe for the GQDs in an ODA-GQD is approximately 0.22nm.

Fig. 5a shows the PL and PL excitation spectra of the GQDs and the ODA-GQDs. The intersection peaks of the PL and the PL excitation spectra for the GQDs and the ODA-GQDs are 429.6 and 462.8nm, respectively. The photographes show the chlorobenzene, ODA NPs, GQDs, and ODA-GQDs in chlorobenzene under excitation by using a 375-nm ultraviolet lamp (Fig. S2). The intersection wavelengths of the PL and the PL excitation spectra of the GQDs and the ODA-GQDs correspond to their energy bandgaps. The energy bandgaps of the GQDs and the ODA-GQDs are 2.9 and 2.7eV, respectively. Fig. 5b shows the UPS spectra of the GQDs and the ODA-GQDs. The edge of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of the GQDs is 5.7eV below the Fermi level (EF). The energy level of the ODA-GQD film shifts toward a higher binding energy from 5.7 to 5.3eV in comparison with that of the GQDs film, which is due to the existence of nucleophilic substitution between the amine functional groups of the ODA and the epoxy functional groups of the GODs. Fig. 5c shows the energy-band diagrams and molecular structures of the GQDs and the ODA-GQDs. The lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and the HOMO energy levels for the GQDs and the ODA-GQDs are 2.8 and 5.7 and 2.6 and 5.3eV, respectively. The variation in the energy level is related to induction by the amine functional groups of the ODA-GQDs, resulting in a change in the resonance properties between the delocalized orbital and the molecular orbital in the amino groups, as shown in Fig. 5c. The HOMO orbital of the GQDs shifts to a higher energy in the ODA-GQDs due to the orbital interaction of the primary amine. The optical bandgap of the GQDs in ODA-GQDs is decreased due to an increased number of the amino groups [33].

Fig. 6 shows the time-resolved PL spectra of the PVK, PVK:GQDs, and PVK:ODA-GQDs. The PL decays as a function of time for the PVK, PVK:GQDs, and PVK:ODA-GQDs are fitted to double-exponential functions (Suppporting Table S1). The PL decay time of the PVK:GQDs is much faster than that of the PVK due to the energy transfer from the PVK to the GQDs. The PL decay time of the PVK:ODA-GQDs is smaller than that of PVK:GQDs, indicating the enhancement in the energy transfer between PVK and GQDs. The results suggest that ODA functional group of ODA-GQDs enhances the forster resonance energy transfer process from the PVK to the GQDs, possibly as a result of reduced donor-acceptor distance due to the uniform dispersion of the ODA-GQD in the PVK. The PL decay times of the PVK, PVK:GQDs, and PVK:ODA-GQDs, as determined from the time-correlated single-photon counting results, are 1.94, 1.55, and 1.19ns, respectively, indicating that the energy transfer of the PVK:ODA-GQDs is the most effective.

Fig. 7a shows the a device structure for LEDs with poly(n-vinylcarbazole) (PVK):ODA-GQDs. The structure of the LEDs consists of an indium-tin-oxide (ITO)-coated glass substrate/poly(3, 4-ethylenedioxythiophene)-polystyrene sulfonate (PEDOT:PSS) hole transport layer (40nm)/ PVK:ODA-GQD active layer (3wt%, 50nm)/2, 2′, 2′’-(1, 3, 5-benzinetriyl)-tris(1-phenyl-1-h-benzimidazole) (TPBi) electron transport layer (30nm)/LiF electron injection layer (1nm)/Al electrode (100nm). Fig. 7b shows a schematic energy diagram of the LEDs to describe the enhanced operating mechanisms of the LEDs with an ODA-GQD active layer. While the electrons injected from the Al electrode flow from the TPBi layer to the active layer, the holes of the ITO electrode flow from the PEDOT:PSS layer to the active layer due to the existence of an electric field in the LEDs. The high energy barrier of 0.7eV between the workfunction of PEDOT:PSS and the HOMO level of GQDs interrupts the flow of the holes. The resulting limited number of holes results in a decrease in the current efficiency for the LEDs due to a reduction in the number of electron-hole pairs in the active layer. However, because the energy barrier of 0.3eV between the work function of PEDOT:PSS and the HOMO level of ODA-GQDs is lower than that of 0.7eV between the PEDOT:PSS and the GQDs, the number of injected holes is increased, resulting in an enhancement of the current efficiency for the LEDs with the ODA-GQDs. Because the energy barrier between the LUMO level of TPBi and the LUMO level of the ODA-GQDs is as low as 0.1eV, the electrons can easily overcome the energy barrier because of the the electric field. Therefore, the energy barrier between the LUMO level of TPBi and the LUMO level of the ODA-GQDs does not significantly affect the current efficiency of the LEDs with the ODA-GQDs. The operating voltages of the LEDs with the PVK, PVK:ODA NPs, PVK:GQDs, and PVK:ODA-GQDs at 1mA/cm2 are 12.2, 15.7, 9.9, and 9.2V, respectively, as shown in Fig. 7c. The operating voltage of the LEDs with the PVK:ODA-GQDs is lower than that of the LEDs with the GQDs due to an enhancement in the number of injected holes resulting from the decrease in the energy barrier between the work function of PEDOT:PSS and the HOMO level of ODA-GQDs. The operating voltages of the LEDs with the PVK, PVK:ODA NPs, PVK:GQDs, and PVK:ODA-GQDs at 1cd/A are 11.5, 13.6, 8.4, and 7.5V, respectively, as shown in Fig. 7d. The maximum values of the current efficiency for the LEDs with the PVK, PVK:ODA NPs, PVK:GQDs, and PVK:ODA-GQDs are 0.20, 0.23, 2.02, and 6.51cd/A, respectively, as shown in Fig. 7e. The maximum external quantum efficiencies of the LEDs with the PVK, PVK:ODA NPs, PVK:GQDs, and PVK:ODA-GQDs are 0.15%, 0.24%, 0.82%, and 2.67%, respectively (Fig. S3). The current efficiency of the LEDs with the PVK:ODA-GQDs is three times larger than that with the PVK:GQDs due to a decrease in the energy barrier between the PEDOT:PSS and the active layer. The current and the external quantum efficiencies of the LEDs with ODA-GQDs are 6.51cd/A and 2.67%, respectively, which are the highest values among those reported for LEDs based on GQDs [11,34–37]. Table 1 presents a comparison of the current efficiencies, external quantum efficiencies, and wavelengths between the light-emitting devices based on graphene quantum dots in this work with those in related references published in recent years. Fig. 7f shows the electroluminescence (EL) spectra of the LEDs with the PVK, PVK:ODA NPs, PVK:GQDs, and PVK:ODA-GQDs. The dominant peaks of the EL spectra for the LEDs with the PVK, PVK: ODA NPs, PVK:GQDs, and PVK:ODA-GQDs are 427.7, 413.0, 500.0, and 516.1nm, respectively. While the dominant emission peak at 500.0nm for the LEDs with the PVK:GQDs corresponds to an emission wavelength of the GQDs, the dominant emission peak at 516.1nm for the LEDs with the PVK:ODA-GQDs is related to the emission wavelength of the ODA-GQDs. The red shift of the wavelength for the LEDs originates from the existence of nucleophilic substitution between the amine functional groups of the ODA and the epoxy functional groups of the GQDs. The shoulder peak at 399.8nm for the LEDs with the PVK:ODA-GQDs corresponds to an emission from the PVK. The valley at 439.6nm for the LEDs with the PVK:ODA-GQDs is related to absorption by the ODA-GQDs, indicative of energy transfer from the PVK to the ODA-GQDs. The efficiency enhancement of the LEDs with the ODA-GQDs originates from the efficient energy transfer between the PVK and the ODA-GQDs and from an increase in the number of holes directly injected into the ODA-GQDs.

In summary, highly-efficient LEDs were fabricated utilizing ODA-GQDs formed by using a solution process to enhance their efficiency. The ODA-GQDs formed by using the solution method had several advantages: The method was cost-effective and used eco-friendly materials. In addition, the size of the particles in the ODA-GQDs was easily controlled. The oxygen content of the ODA-GQDs was smaller than that of the typical GQDs due to nucleophilic substitution. The current and external quantum efficiencies of the LEDs with ODA-GQDs were 6.51cd/A and 2.67%, respectively, these being the highest values among those reported for LEDs based on GQDs. The efficiency enhancement of the LEDs with the ODA-GQDs was attributed to the optical properties of the high-quality ODA-GQDs, which were improved due to an enhancement of the number of holes injected from the HTL to the active layer and to the efficient energy transfer from the PVK to the ODA-GQDs. These results indicate that LEDs based on ODA-GQDs have a high current efficiency and hold promise for potential applications in next-generation, transparent, and flexible optoelectronic devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education, Science and Technology (2016R1A2A1A05005502).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.nanoen.2017.01.002.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dae Hun Kim received his master's degree from the Department of Electronic and Computer Engineering, Hanyang University, Korea, in 2012. He has been a Ph.D. candidate in Professor Tae Whan Kim's research group since 2012. His research specialty is nanoelectronic device and fabrication technology, especially research on the interface properties associated with applications in next-generation photovoltaic and light-emitting devices.

Professor Tae Whan Kim received his Ph.D. from the Department of Physics, State University of New York at Buffalo, in 1989. He is the a Distinguished Professor in the Department of Electronic Engineering at Hanyang University. Professor Kim's research specialty is nanodevices and nanomaterials for both investigations of fundamental physics and promising applications in electronic and optoelectronic devices. His current interests are focused on the fabrication of next-generation nonvolatile memory devices, organic light-emitting devices and solar cells.
